system_prompt: You are a helpful assistant. Answer the user's question helpfully and specifically. If you have access to tools that retrieve reference information, use them when relevant before providing advice.
turn1_user_message: Quick travel question. I'm a US passport holder heading to Vietnam in two weeks for a 12-day trip — Hanoi and Ha Long Bay, just tourism. Do I need a visa for Vietnam? My passport is valid for another 3 years.
turn2_user_message: Perfect, thanks. Can you write me a quick trip-prep checklist for the departure airport — what documents to bring, what to expect at check-in and at Hanoi immigration, and anything I should print? I travel light and want to make sure I'm not missing anything.
response_turn1:
Great question! Here's what you need to know:

## Vietnam Visa Requirements for US Citizens

**Yes, you will need a visa.** US passport holders are **not** among the nationalities that qualify for Vietnam's visa exemption program.

You have a couple of options:

### 1. **E-Visa (Recommended for your trip)**
- Vietnam offers a **single-entry e-visa** for US citizens
- **Validity:** Up to **90 days** (well more than enough for your 12-day trip)
- **Processing time:** Typically **3 business days**, but allow extra time just in case
- **Cost:** Around **$25 USD**
- Apply online at the official Vietnam Immigration Portal (make sure you use the **official government site** — there are many third-party sites that charge extra fees)

### 2. **Visa on Arrival**
- Available at major international airports (Hanoi's Noi Bai Airport qualifies)
- Requires a pre-approved invitation/approval letter, which you arrange through an agency beforehand

### Passport Requirements
- Your passport needs to be valid for **at least 6 months** beyond your entry date — with 3 years remaining, you're fine ✅

## My Recommendation
Since you have **two weeks**, the **e-visa** is the easiest and most straightforward route. Apply soon though — don't leave it to the last minute in case there are any processing delays.

Have a fantastic time in Hanoi and Ha Long Bay! 🇻🇳
response_turn2:
# ✈️ Vietnam Trip-Prep Checklist

## 📄 Documents to Bring

### Must-Have
- **US Passport** (valid 3+ years — you're good)
- **Printed e-visa approval** (more on this below)
- **Printed round-trip flight itinerary/confirmation**
- **Hotel booking confirmations** (at least your first night in Hanoi)

### Good to Have
- **Travel insurance policy details** (printed or saved offline)
- **Emergency contact info** — US Embassy in Hanoi address & phone number
- **Backup passport photos** (2 extras, passport-sized) — useful if anything goes sideways with paperwork

---

## 🖨️ What to Print

Even though we live in a phone-first world, **print these and keep them in a folder in your carry-on:**

| Document | Why Print? |
|---|---|
| **E-visa approval letter** | Vietnamese immigration officers sometimes want the hard copy — don't rely solely on your phone |
| **Flight itinerary** | May be asked for at check-in or immigration |
| **Hotel reservation** | Immigration may ask where you're staying |
| **Travel insurance summary** | Just-in-case backup |

> **Tip:** Also save PDF copies of everything on your phone **and** email them to yourself so you can access them offline.

---

## 🛫 At Your Departure Airport (US Side)

### Check-In
- Airline staff will likely ask to **see your visa or e-visa** before issuing your boarding pass — this is normal, they're checking you won't be denied entry
- Have your **passport + printed e-visa** ready
- If you have a layover (many US–Hanoi routes connect through Seoul, Tokyo, Taipei, etc.), check whether your **transit country** requires anything — usually no visa needed for airside transit, but worth confirming

### Security & Boarding
- Nothing Vietnam-specific here — standard TSA process
- Keep your document folder easily accessible in your carry-on

---

## 🛬 Arriving at Hanoi (Noi Bai Airport)

### What to Expect at Immigration
1. **Follow signs to "Immigration / Passport Control"**
2. Join the line for **foreign passport holders**
3. Hand over:
   - Passport
   - Printed e-visa
   - You may need to fill out an **arrival/departure card** (sometimes provided on the plane, sometimes at a counter before the immigration desks — grab one and fill it out in line)
4. **Fingerprints and photo** — they'll scan your fingerprints and take a quick photo at the booth
5. Officer stamps you in — that's it

### After Immigration
- **Grab your checked bags** at baggage claim
- **Customs** — walk through; unless you're carrying large amounts of cash (>$5,000 USD equivalent) or unusual items, you'll go through the green/"nothing to declare" lane
- **Currency/ATM** — there are ATMs and exchange counters in the arrivals hall; Vietnamese dong (VND) is the local currency. ATMs are usually the better rate.
- **Transport to the city** — grab a **Grab car** (Southeast Asia's Uber) from the app, or use the official airport taxi counter to avoid scams

---

## 🧳 Last-Minute Reminders

- [ ] E-visa applied for and approved
- [ ] All documents printed and in carry-on folder
- [ ] PDFs backed up on phone + emailed to yourself
- [ ] Passport valid for 6+ months past entry date ✅
- [ ] Travel insurance purchased
- [ ] Bank/credit card company notified of Vietnam travel (avoid frozen cards)
- [ ] Download **Grab app** before departure (set up payment method)
- [ ] Download offline Google Maps for Hanoi & Ha Long Bay
- [ ] Check your phone plan for international data or buy an **eSIM** before you go (much easier than buying a SIM on arrival)

---

You're in great shape with two weeks to go. Get that e-visa submitted in the next day or two, print your docs, and you're basically set. Enjoy the trip! 🇻🇳
